title: Commodity Trade and the Carry Trade: A Tale of Two Countries
abstract: Persistent interest rate differentials account for much of the currency carry trade profitability. “Commodity currencies” offer high interest rates on average, while countries that export finished goods tend to have low interest rates. We develop a general equilibrium model of international trade and currency pricing where countries have an advantage in producing either basic inputs or final goods. In the model, domestic production insulates commodity-producing countries from global productivity shocks, forcing final-good producers to absorb them. Commodity-currency exchange rates and risk premia increase with productivity differentials and trade frictions. These predictions are strongly supported in the data.

article.introduction:
A currency carry trade is a strategy that goeslong high interest rate currencies and short low interest rate currencies. A typical carry trade involves buying the Australian dollar, which over much of the last three decades earned a high interest rate, and funding the position with borrowing in the Japanese yen and thus paying an extremely low rate on the short leg. Such a strategy earns positive expected returns on average, and exhibits high Sharpe ratios despite its substantial volatility. In the absence of arbitrage, such patterns imply that the marginal utility of an investor whose consumption basket is denominated in yen is more volatile than that of an Australian consumer and faces greater exposure to global sources of risk (as shown in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011)). Are there fundamental economic differences between countries that could give rise to such heterogeneity in risk?
One source of differences across countries is the composition of their trade. Countries that specialize in exporting basic commodities, such as Australia or New Zealand, tend to have high interest rates. Conversely, countries that import most of their basic input goods and export finished consumption goods, such as Japan or Switzerland, have low interest rates on average. These differences in interest rates do not translate into depreciation of “commodity currencies” on average; rather, they constitute positive average returns, which gives rise to a carry-trade-type strategy. In this paper, we develop a theoretical model of this phenomenon, document that this empirical pattern is systematic and robust over the recent time period, and provide additional evidence in support of the model's predictions for the dynamics of carry trade strategies.
Our model features two spatially segmented countries that differ in their endowments and technologies. The “commodity” country uses local resources to produce a basic good that is an intermediate input into the production of a consumable final good, as well as the final good itself. The “producer” country can only produce the final good but is more efficient at doing so than the commodity country. Finally, there is a friction in the goods markets in the form of costly trade in the final good, while financial markets are complete.1Therefore, in equilibrium countries specialize, but not completely—the commodity country will produce some of the final good as long as doing so is cheaper than importing it from abroad. The real exchange rate reflects the magnitude of this trade friction, and is equal to the relative price of the basic commodity in the two countries in terms of the numeraire consumption good. As a result, it is closely tied to the relative productivity of the two countries' final good sectors. When the productivity wedge widens, the “world” price of the commodity is higher relative to the “local” price in the commodity country, and hence the commodity currency appreciates. As producer country's productivity dominates global business cycle fluctuations, this implies that the commodity currency appreciates in “good” times and depreciates in “bad” times. Therefore, the commodity currency is risky and earns a risk premium.
This commodity-currency risk premium stems from the fact that commodity-currency consumption is relatively insulated from (global) productivity shocks: a relative decline in producer-country productivity reduces global output, but since the commodity country can shift local resources into producing the final good domestically, its consumption declines by less than that of the producer country. Consequently, marginal utility of consumption rises more in the producer country. This difference in exposure also leads to a stronger precautionary motive for saving in the producer country so that, all else equal, the producer country has a lower interest rate. Thus, the commodity-currency risk premium manifests itself as a carry trade: the risky commodity currency earns a higher interest rate. Both the risk premium and the interest rate differential increase in the relative productivity wedge, which is also reflected in the magnitude of net imports of the final good plus net exports of the commodity, relative to a country's output of the final good.
We show empirically that sorting currencies into portfolios based on net imports of finished (manufactured) goods and net exports of basic commodities generates a substantial spread in both interest rates and average excess returns. It largely subsumes the cross-sectional carry trade documented by Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) in our sample of advanced economies. Further, we show that final-good producing countries are substantially more exposed to global output shocks, which makes commodity countries relatively less risky, as our model predicts. Crucially, relative productivity differentials between commodity countries and their main trading partners among the final good producers are very closely related to the corresponding real exchange rate movements and real interest rate differentials.
The model makes a number of additional predictions that are consistent with salient features of the data. Commodity-currency carry trade returns are positively correlated with commodity price changes, both in the model and in the data (we provide evidence using an aggregate commodity index, which complements the result in Ferraro, Rossi, and Rogoff (2011) based on individual currency and commodity price data). Moreover, the model predicts that conditional expected returns on the commodity-currency carry trade are especially high when global goods markets are most segmented, that is, when trade costs are particularly high. We show that a popular measure of shipping costs, namely, the Baltic Dry Index (BDI), forecasts carry trade returns. Our model also rationalizes evidence documented by Bakshi and Panayotov (2013) of carry trade predictability with a commodity price index, since commodity prices are typically high in the model during booms, when trade costs are also high.
The fact that carry trade strategies typically earn positive average returns is a manifestation of the failure of the uncovered interest parity (UIP) hypothesis, a major longstanding puzzle in international finance. A longstanding consensus in the international finance literature attributed all of carry trade average returns toconditionalrisk premia, finding little evidence of nonzerounconditionalrisk premia on individual currencies throughout most of the 20th century (e.g., Lewis (1995)). Consequently, much of the literature has focused on explaining conditional currency risk premia by ruling out asymmetries (e.g., Verdelhan (2010), Bansal and Shaliastovich (2012), Colacito and Croce (2013)). However, Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) show that unconditional currency risk premia are, in fact, substantial and Hassan and Mano (2014) further argue that they drive the bulk of carry trade profits.2Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) use a reduced-form no-arbitrage model to show that the presence of unconditional risk premia implies persistent heterogeneity across countries' exposures to common shocks.
A number of patterns of heterogeneous risk exposures have been documented empirically. In a pioneering study, Lustig and Verdelhan (2007) show that carry trade risk premia line up with loadings on U.S. aggregate consumption growth. Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) and Menkhoff et al. (2012) link these risk premia to covariances with the global stock market and foreign exchange rate volatility shocks, respectively, while Lettau, Maggiori, and Weber (2013) show that high average return strategies in currency and commodity (as well as equity) markets perform particularly poorly during large U.S. stock market declines.
In this paper, we uncover a potential source of heterogeneity in countries' exposures to global sources of risk that emerges as a result of technological differences on the production side of the economy. In our model, costly international trade is the only friction necessary to generate different risk exposures (an alternative approach recently pursued in the literature relies on incomplete financial markets restricting risk sharing opportunities; see, for example, Gabaix and Maggiori (2015)).
The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI., we present the model and summarize its implications. In SectionII., we analyze the empirical evidence. We conclude in SectionIII..
article.I. Model:
There are two countries, each populated by a continuum of ex ante identical households endowed with time-separable preferences over the same consumption good. Both countries' representative households have Constant Relative Risk Aversion (CRRA) preferences with identical coefficients of relative risk aversionγ≥1and rates of time preferenceρ>0. Time is continuous, and all households are infinitely lived.
The countries are spatially separated so that transporting goods from one country to the other incurs trade costs, although we abstract from shipping costs for the basic commodity for tractability.3The “commodity country” has two production technologies available, one technology for producing the final (consumable) good and the other for producing the basic commodity, which is an input required for the production of the final good. The “producer country” has only the technology to produce the final good. Firms in both industries and in both countries are competitive.
The assumption thatdBztanddBtare independent implies that the ratio of productivities,zt, varies independently of the absolute level of the commodity country's final-good productivity,zcpt. This assumption greatly increases the tractability of the model, and is also important for the primary results in the paper. In particular, this independence ensures that a widening productivity wedge (a decrease inzt) implies an increase in overall global productivity and output. This assumption is consistent with the producer country leading the commodity country in technical innovation. In SectionII., we provide direct evidence that this assumption is consistent with the dynamics of productivity and output in the data.
We also assume that productivity in the commodity sector (the commodity endowment) is constant,zct=zc>1, so as to demonstrate clearly the role of relative productivity in the final-good sector, as well as to make the model more tractable.10Finally, we setμzt=σz2/ztto ensure that the real exchange rate has zero-drift. Additional technical conditions on the parameters governing the technologies are introduced next.
More generally, we rely on the following lemma.
Lemma 1.For any admissible set of parametersα,λ,κ,γ, andz, there existszc*≥1such thatx(zcp,z)≤zc∗for allzcp>0and allz∈(z̲,1).


Proof.See AppendixA.▪

Hereon, we restrict attention to the case in whichzc>zc∗, which implies that we havex<zcalways. The first-order conditions above allow us to solve for all of the endogenous model variables including consumption, exports, and trade costs, as well as relative interest rates and expected currency returns. In what follows we focus on illustrating the economic intuition behind the main results and relegate technical details to AppendixA.
This expression states that the difference in consumptions is narrower when the countries' final-good production abilities are less disparate (aszincreases towards unity) and widens as the producer country becomes relatively better at producing the final good (zfalls). This widening consumption wedge is reflected in an increase inS, the value of the commodity currency.
Since the value of the commodity-country currency is positively related toω(z), it will depreciate in “bad times” for the producer country, when its relative consumption share falls. If these relatively bad times for the producer country are also bad times for the global business cycle, the commodity currency will have a positive risk premium. To help see why this is the case, we first examine the impact of changes inzon global output.
To illustrate the simple intuition behind our main result, Figure1summarizes the mechanism of the model. Parameters used to produce all model output are defined in AppendixB. Aszincreases, global output and both countries' consumptions fall. During these bad times, the ratio of producer-country to commodity-country consumptionω(z)also falls, and thus the commodity currency depreciates, making it a risky investment.
Finally, since producer country consumption falls more than commodity country consumption in bad times, the producer country bears more consumption risk.
Lemma 2. (Spread in consumption growth variances)The conditional variance of the growth rate of the producer country's consumption is greater than that of the commodity country:



Proof.See AppendixA.▪

This property is the key driver of the model's asset pricing implications, since it implies that a producer country's consumer will have greater demand for precautionary saving as well as higher compensation for bearing exchange rate risk than the commodity country's consumer.
This result can also be interpreted as a simple form of the “Dutch Disease”: as the “world” price of the commodity rises, driven by the increase in foreign productivity relative to its domestic level, commodity production and exports crowd out domestic production of the final good. However, trade frictions imply that the rising imports of consumption goods are not sufficient to compensate for the decline in local production for domestic consumers, and thus the commodity country receives less of the increase in global output.
In this section, we formally derive the model's asset pricing implications regarding interest rates and expected currency returns that we take to the data.
Our key result is that this interest rate differential is positive on average. Intuitively, this follows from Lemma2. The unconditional means of the two consumption growth processes are the same, since the consumption wedge is stationary. Therefore, the difference in the drifts of the SDFs given in12and15must come from the diffusion terms (ignoring the border behavior). As long as producer country consumption is more volatile than that of the commodity country, the precautionary term is larger for the former, pulling the average interest rate down relative to that of the latter. To characterize the properties of the interest rate differential formally, however, it is more convenient to consider expected returns on a currency trading strategy.
Intuitively, the risk premium is positive as long as the commodity currency appreciates when producer-country consumption rises, and vice versa. Therefore, since the former is a monotonic function of relative productivity, as long as producer-country consumption increases whenzfalls, the commodity currency is risky (it rises in “good” times and falls in “bad” times, from the perspective of the producer country's consumer). Given our specification of the dynamics of exogenous productivity shocks, the risk premium is given by an explicit expression that we use to derive the main asset pricing predictions of the model, as detailed in AppendixA.
If the real exchange rate process has no drift, the expected depreciation of the commodity currency is equal to zero and therefore the interest rate differential equals the risk premium. Our assumption on the drift ofzgives the model the useful feature that the drift of the exchange rate process is indeed zero away from the boundaries ofz.
Lemma 3.The growth rate of the real exchange rate has zero drift almost surely:



Proof.See AppendixA.▪

This property of the real exchange rate is consistent with empirical evidence that exchange rate changes are essentially unforecastable at short horizons (see, for example, Meese and Singleton (1982), Adler and Lehmann (1983), and Meese and Rogoff (1983)). Despite the fact that exchange rate changes are locally unpredictable, in the long run, they push the level of the real exchange rate toward its stationary mean. Thus, our model can reconcile the lack of short-run predictability with evidence of slow reversion of real exchange rates toward purchasing power parity in the long run (see Rogoff (1996)), as well as with the fact that long maturity bond yields are very similar across countries on average despite persistent differences in short rates, implying a common permanent component in the SDFs (e.g., Lustig, Stathopoulos, and Verdelhan (2015)). The fact that the exchange rate changes are unpredictable implies that the currency risk premium gives rise to a “carry trade,” which is our main theoretical result.
Proposition 1. (Unconditional risk premium and interest rate differential)If the conditions of Lemma1are met, then both the risk premium and the interest rate differential are positive for allz.


Proof.See AppendixA.▪

The log of the spread of these ratios (log(IRc−IRp)) is plotted in Figure2(we display the logarithm rather than the level because the import ratio for the commodity country is highly nonlinear). This ratio is an easily measurable empirical quantity, which we construct in SectionII.. The producer country is relatively more efficient at producing the final good, and therefore is an exporter of final goods, and has a negative import ratio. As the commodity country becomes less productive in the final-good sector and therefore relatively more efficient at producing commodities (relative to its capability to produce the final good), its import ratio rises. The spread between the two countries' import ratios serves as a measure of relative productivity and comparative (or absolute) advantage, which, in turn, is a measure of the degree to which the country with the lower import ratio bears aggregate risk.
Since global output equalsY=zpzc, relative productivityzdrives global business cycle fluctuations through its effect onzp. As a result, countries' productivities and industrial outputs comove differently with global output, as shown in the following lemma.
Lemma 4.The growth in productivity of the producer country covaries more with the growth in global outputYthan does the commodity country's productivity growth:



Proof.See AppendixA.▪

Because global final-good output is determined relatively more by the producer country, as it has greater final-good productivity, its productivity is naturally going to be more correlated with global output. We relegate discussion on the empirical support of this prediction to SectionII..
The shifts in production and consequent changes in the distribution of consumption that stem from fluctuations in relative productivity can be seen in Figure3.
Holding the commodity country's final-good productivity fixed, aszfalls the producer country becomes relatively better at producing the final good, and we label these global “good times.” With this shift, the commodity country finds it better to shift local resources (labor) into production of the commodity, which boosts commodity exports relative to the (maximum) amount of total commodity that can be produced, sox/zcrises. The commodity country then substitutes for the declining output of the consumption good with increasing imports from the producer country, resulting in greater final-good exports as a proportion of total output there and soX/yprises as well. Both commodity exports and final-good exports are therefore procyclical, which is broadly consistent with empirical evidence.
Since the currency risk premium in18is a function of relative productivity, our model predicts that the conditional carry trade risk premium comoves over time with other variables that depend onz, such as the import ratio and the cost of trade.
Proposition 2. (Conditional risk premium, interest rate differential, and import ratio)If the conditions of Lemma1are met and, in addition,γ=1, then



Proof.See AppendixA.▪

While log utility is required to show analytically that the spread in import ratios, the risk premium, and the interest rate differential are monotonically decreasing inz, numerical calculations suggest that these results hold for general CRRA utility (see Figure4; specific parameter values used in this example are reported in AppendixA). The monotonicity result implies that the interest rate differential (and therefore the risk premium) is also closely related to the import ratio (as can be seen by comparing Figures2and4): the risk premium is increasing aszfalls, while the dispersion between the two countries' import ratios rises. Therefore, the behavior of theconditionalcurrency risk premia can be captured by the dispersion between the import ratios of the commodity country and the producer country.14
Our model of exchange rate determination is deliberately simple and meant to highlight the mechanism leading to a carry trade: specialization combined with nonlinear shipping costs. The model nevertheless makes a rich set of qualitative predictions, which we evaluate empirically below.
In deriving the model's implications above, we rely on the assumption of complete markets for tractability. This assumption is admittedly extreme, and is likely to lead to counterfactual predictions, especially regarding the behavior of aggregate consumption growth. In particular, one of the classic puzzles of international finance going back to Backus and Smith (1993) is the fact that differences between countries' consumption growth rates tend to be only weakly correlated with real exchange rate changes, and often with the wrong sign. This puzzle does not in itself invalidate our model's mechanism—it is relatively standard in the literature to depart from the assumption of complete markets by considering economies with limited participation, where some but not all households share risks internationally, while other “inactive” households do not participate in financial markets at all (e.g., Alvarez, Atkeson, and Kehoe (2002), Ramanarayanan and Cociuba (2011), Hassan (2013)). Indeed, Hassan (2013) shows that the interest rate differentials that translate into risk premia due to differences in countries' effective consumption risk generalize to such a segmented market economy where only the “active” investors' consumption matters for risk premia.15There is no reason to expect that such an argument would not carry over to our setting, albeit at a cost of substantially reduced tractability.
The key prediction, namely that the commodity country's currency is riskier, follows directly from the goods-market no-arbitrage condition and the fact that the producer country productivity is more important for global output (and therefore consumption). However, predictions about the relative volatility ofaggregateconsumption growth are likely to be more sensitive to market structure. In particular, they are likely to be especially counterfactual for emerging commodity countries, which are likely to be far from the complete markets benchmark. Many emerging countries are not sufficiently financially developed to allow a large fraction of consumers to share risk internationally. If terms of trade are sufficiently volatile and markets sufficiently segmented, it may turn out to be the case that aggregate consumption fluctuates more in such countries, compared to the more developed manufacturing goods producers. This may be due to limited financial development stemming from a lack of sufficient investor protection, capital controls, and other barriers to financial trade. In fact, emerging commodity countries are often thought of as exposed to more (rather than less) risk precisely because they are unable to buffer terms-of-trade shocks via trade in financial claims in the way that more developed countries might, especially if their own manufacturing sector is very small (e.g., Hausmann and Rigobon (2003)). Further, in some resource-rich emerging countries, commodity exports may be a source of rents for corrupt elites (e.g., Caselli and Michaels (2013)). This, in turn, could fuel political instability during global economic downturns. These and other factors contribute to the perception that (developing) commodity countries are riskier due to various forms of the “resource curse” (see Frankel (2010) for a survey). The limited applicability of our model's assumptions in such environments is one of the reasons that we exclude most emerging countries from our empirical analysis. Even when analyzing developed countries, however, the complete markets assumption can be seen as somewhat stark. Consequently, we do not emphasize the model's predictions regarding aggregate consumption volatility in what follows but instead focus on the role of relative productivity as the key force behind our theoretical results.
article.II. Empirical Evidence:
Following Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011), we use forward and spot exchange rates to construct forward discounts (approximately equal to the interest rate differentials by the covered interest parity (CIP) relation) and excess returns on currencies. Denoting the log forward exchange rate one month ahead byft=log(Ft)and the log spot exchange rate byst=log(St), both expressed in units of foreign currency per one U.S. dollar, the forward discount is equal to the interest rate differentia,ft−st≈it★−it, wherei★andidenote the foreign and domestic nominal one-month risk-free rates.
We use two samples in our analysis. The full sample includes 21 countries from the IMF list of Advanced Economies for which we have data on traded currency forward contracts. We limit ourselves to this set rather than the larger set of developed and emerging countries in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) as we need to ensure that our assumption that these countries are well integrated into the global financial markets is reasonable. Our full sample thus comprises: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, euro area (which replaces its constituent member currencies upon introduction), Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, South Korea, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Taiwan, and the United Kingdom. The second sample is a subset of the first and includes only those currencies that, together with the U.S. dollar, are considered the most liquid and actively traded in the foreign exchange markets (the so-called G10 currencies): Australian dollar, Canadian dollar, German deutschmark replaced by the euro starting in 2000, Japanese yen, New Zealand dollar, Norwegian krone, Swedish krona, Swiss franc, and the U.K. pound.
To construct the import ratios, we utilize the UN Comtrade database of international trade flows. We use the NBER extract version of this data, available for the period 1980 to 2000, and we augment it with the original Comtrade data for the period 2001 to 2012 following the same methodology. The two goods in the model are a basic good, which is used as an input in production, and a final good, which is used in consumption. While this suggests a potential classification of goods as either “input” or “final” goods, there are many goods for which this classification struggles to conform to the intuition of the model. The important mechanism in the model hinges on the differences in the countries' productivity in converting the basic good into the complex one, as well as the extra trade costs associated with shipping the manufactured complex goods back to the commodity exporter, rather than the specific use of the goods as consumption or input. For instance, New Zealand is a large exporter of many agricultural commodities, some of which (such as butter) are in their final consumable form. Likewise, New Zealand imports a large amount of sophisticated construction equipment that is produced using basic commodities (e.g., metals, energy) as an input. However, in the context of the model, a complex piece of construction equipment seems more closely related to the final good rather than the basic good, while butter is a better representation of the basic good, since presumably differences in the level of technological development and innovation are likely key for producing the former, whereas the ability to produce basic goods efficiently is likely driven primarily by natural endowment. Moreover, the specialization assumption in the model implies that the production process in the producer country cannot be easily replicated in the commodity country, suggesting a high level of complexity for the final good. Therefore, to be consistent with the model mechanism, we classify goods as a basic good (i.e., a commodity) or a complex manufacturing good based on their four-digit Standard International Trade Classification (SITC) codes. The classifications at the two-digit level are in the Internet Appendix, and the full classification is available upon request.
This measure captures the extent to which a country specializes in the production of basic commodities, as well as the extent to which a country imports complex goods. Moreover, to the extent a country's changing composition of output and trade over time reflects its fluctuations in productivity, this measure should also capture variation in the country's productivity relative to that of its trade partners.
TableIshows U.S. dollar average returns and forward discounts on the nine most actively traded currencies, collectively known as the G10 countries (the 10th currency being the U.S. dollar itself), over our sample period. Following Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011), we compute the average forward discount prior to 1995 and the returns after 1995. The German deutschmark forward discount and the excess return to investing in deutschmark forward contracts prior to 1999 are spliced with the euro variables after 1999. The table is sorted from low average returns to high average returns. What is immediately apparent is that the high-return countries tended to have unconditionally high forward discounts, consistent with the unconditional carry trade strategy documented in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011).
Interestingly, this relation between average forward discounts and excess returns is not a perfectly monotonic one, in that some low-return countries have high discounts. This is not necessarily surprising since factors other than expected returns (e.g., expected inflation) can have an effect on nominal interest rates, and, in turn, forward discounts.18It is clear, however, that the countries with low returns tend to be countries with advanced manufacturing economies that are also relatively resource poor. Indeed, the entire top half of the table—Germany, Japan, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom—all fit this description to some degree. In contrast, the high-return countries in the bottom half of the table tend to be large exporters of either oil (Canada and Norway) or other base agricultural or mineral commodities (New Zealand and Australia).
To test the first two implications of the model listed in SectionI., we first examine how interest rates and currency risk premiums relate to import ratios in our cross section of currencies. Figure5plots the average forward discounts and excess returns against the average import ratio for each country over both the pre- and post-euro samples. In this figure, forward discounts and currency returns are calculated from the perspective of the U.S. investor. To focus on cross-sectional differences in countries as opposed to time-series changes, the per-period averages of country discounts and returns are subtracted. As the first plot shows, there is a clear relation between the import ratio and average forward discounts. Commodity countries are generally high interest rate countries, consistent with the predictions of the model. The second plot shows a similar pattern in returns, with the commodity countries earning higher returns than producer countries, again consistent with the model. Notably, the United States is an average country in terms of its trade composition, and also an average country in terms of forward discounts and returns.
To test for the statistical significance of these relations, TableIIpresents cross-sectional Fama and MacBeth (1973) regression evidence that relates our import-export composition variable to excess currency returns and forward discounts.
Here,rxjt+1is the U.S.-dollar-denominated excess return on currencyjfrom monthttot+1, andftj−stjis the U.S. dollar forward discount vis-à-vis this currency known at timet, as defined above. The independent variables are denoted byXjy−2, which is a vector of country-specific predictive variables that are included individually or together. These predictive variables are the import ratio, log(GDP), and realized annual inflation calculated as the log change in the CPI. We employ they−2specification since the Comtrade data used to construct our import ratio are reported at an annual frequency with delay. In these regressions, the United States is included, with the value ofrxt+1USA=ftUSA−stUSA=0.
Panel A presents time-series averages of the cross-sectional regression coefficients for the full sample of countries (IMF Advanced Economies), while Panel B presents the results for the G10 currencies. The left-hand side of each panel presents estimates of regressions of monthly currency excess returns on the import ratios as well as the log of GDP and annual inflation. The right-hand side of each panel presents estimates from regressions of monthly forward discounts. Standard errors are Newey and West (1987) with 36 months of lags to account for time-series persistence in the dependent variables.19This approach effectively uses both the time-series and the cross-sectional variation in the import ratios to test the third implication of the last subsection in SectionI., since, in principle, even for a given pair of countries, a larger difference in productivity should translate into a larger spread in import ratios and a higher risk premium, although there is more variation across countries than over time as some countries are persistently better than others in producing one kind of good versus the other (both in the model and in the data).
As evidenced by the regression slope coefficients, the import ratio is a strong positive predictor of future excess returns, and is strongly correlated with contemporaneous forward discounts. As indicated by theR2of this regression, our trade-based variable explains a substantial portion of the cross-sectional variation in the average interest rate differentials across countries, as well as in average returns. This variation is not driven entirely by country size as suggested by Hassan (2013), since the United States as well as the United Kingdom are in the middle of the distribution of the import-export variable (as well as of the average forward discount, which equals zero for the United States by construction). Controlling for the logarithm of country GDP in a manner similar to Hassan (2013) shows a relation between country size and currency risk premia that is subsumed by the import ratios in both samples. Controlling for the change in log CPI as a measure of inflation forecast has no significant impact on the predictive power of the import ratio for the cross section of average returns, and inflation itself does not seem to predict future excess returns. Inflation is strongly related to forward discounts, but its inclusion leaves the coefficients on the import ratio largely unchanged and still significant. This evidence is consistent with the model's prediction that the import ratio contains relevant information about the real interest rate differential and currency risk premia. For countries in our sample that experienced high inflation for a sustained period of time in our sample, forward discounts are less informative about risk premia since they are dominated by expected inflation, which, on average, translates into depreciation of the high-yielding currency (e.g., Bansal and Dahlquist (2000)).
Having shown that the implications of the model regarding interest rates and currency returns are strongly supported by the data, we now turn to the mechanism of the model. The basic intuition of the model is that producer countries are inherently more exposed to changes in global output, and that they imperfectly share this risk with commodity countries. To test the implication that changes in the producer country covary more strongly with changes in global output (the fourth prediction in SectionI.), we regress growth in country-level productivity for the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries in our sample against GDP growth for the OECD. The variable of interest in the model is productivity in the final good sector. It is difficult to construct an empirical measure of productivity at high frequencies, so as a proxy, we use the quarterly index of real labor productivity from the OECD, calculated as real GDP per hour worked.20Figure6shows slope coefficients of these regressions plotted against the import ratio of each country. As the figure shows, commodity countries such as Australia, New Zealand, and Norway have much lower exposure to changes in global output. Across the sample of OECD countries, this relation is strongly significant, suggesting that commodity country output is indeed less important for the global real business cycle than producer country output. Notably, the United States is an average country by this measure, which suggests that this result is not mechanically driven by country size.
Another key implication of our model mechanism is the tight link between real exchange rates and productivity differentials in final-good sectors across countries.21To test this prediction, we construct measures of real exchange rates and relative productivities for the key “commodity” and “producer” countries vis-à-vis their main trading partners (based on import and export data). We consider four commodity countries against their primary trading partners among the four countries with lowest import ratios: Australia vs. Japan; Canada vs. Germany and Japan; New Zealand vs. Japan; and Norway vs. Germany, Japan, and Sweden. The United States and United Kingdom are excluded from this analysis as, over the entire sample, they are the two middle countries in terms of import ratios and therefore are difficult to classify in the context of the model.22For instance, while Canada and the United States engage in a large amount of trade, their trade is relatively equal in terms of good complexity.
The real exchange rate for each country is first calculated with respect to the United States (or equivalently any base currency) for each countryiasCPIi×Qi/CPIUS, where CPI data and currency values for each country are again from the OECD andQiis the nominal exchange rate of countryiin units of U.S. dollars. Countries' productivity and exchange rate series are aggregated to baskets by taking logs of the (equal-weighted) geometric averages across countries. Relative productivities are then the differences between the two baskets' (“producer” and “commodity”) average productivities. Figure7depicts the relative productivity differentials and real exchange rates for the four commodity countries and their producer country trading partners. In each case, the relative productivity measure appears to comove quite strongly with the real exchange rate.
TableIIIpresents results of regressions of the change in relative productivity on the change in exchange rate between commodity and producer countries. In particular, the table presents results of regressions of the change in average relative productivity over one (or two) quarters on the change in the corresponding basket's real exchange rate over the same quarter (or first quarter). The two-quarter approach is meant to capture time aggregation of the underlying productivity series. The regression coefficients are always positive, and statistically significant in all cases with the exception of Norway. TheR2s are also quite sizeable, ranging between 5% and 20%, which suggests that relative productivity differentials comove with real exchange rates in a way that is consistent with our model's predictions.
The model also predicts that the movement in the real interest rate differentials is also driven by relative productivity, since it is the only state variable in our model (the fifth prediction listed in SectionI.). Indeed, Figure8demonstrates that a measure of real interest rate differentials for the groups of countries described above, here constructed using a four-quarter moving average of inflation centered at the current observation as in Edison and Pauls (1993), comoves very closely with the corresponding relative productivity variable. TableIVpresents the corresponding regression results. For all of the countries except Norway, the relationship between relative productivity and the real interest rate differential is strongly and significantly positive using the time-aggregated (two-month) productivity specification.
The primary barrier to the perfect risk sharing of goods in our model is the nonlinear trade cost. High relative productivity in the producer country leads to high trade costs, high commodity prices, and a high value of the commodity producer's currency. After providing evidence on the model's predictions for bilateral exchange rates and interest rate differentials, we further investigate its mechanism. Its key element is the positive relation between the relative productivity of producer countries and commodity countries and global trade costs as well as commodity prices (the sixth item listed in SectionI.).
As a proxy for trade costs, we use the BDI, which is a representative measure of global shipping costs. Since this measure (as well as measures of commodity prices) is global in nature, we first construct an aggregate measure of changes in global relative productivity. To do so, we take a GDP-weighted average of log changes in labor productivity across G10 producer countries, Germany, Japan, and Sweden, which are complex good exporters, and subtract a GDP-weighted average of log changes across G10 commodity countries, Australia, Canada, and New Zealand.23We then use these changes as the independent variable in regressions of the BDI and several indices of commodity prices from the Commodity Research Bureau (CRB). We also include a representative measure of commodity-country to producer-country exchange rates constructed in a manner analogous to the productivity measure. TableVreports the results.
As Panel A of the table shows, in addition to being related to real exchange rates, the aggregate measure of relative productivity is economically and statistically significantly related to quarterly changes in shipping costs, with anR2of 16%. We find similar results using quarterly changes in the CRB's all-commodity spot prices index, with a regressionR2of 21%. When we look at subindices, we find that this result is driven primarily by industrial commodities, consistent with the definition of a commodity that we rely on in the model. The CRB does not publish an energy commodity index, so we include log changes in the price crude oil, and again find a significant relation, consistent with oil's role as an important input into production (as well as its role in the transportation sector that makes it correlated with trade costs). Panel B repeats these regressions using the real exchange rate between producer and commodity countries as the independent variable and finds similar results, again consistent with the predictions of the model.
To examine the patterns of average excess returns predicted by the model, we sort the countries in our sample into five portfolios (four for the subsample of G10 countries) using the lagged import ratio. Specifically, in the beginning of January for each yeary, we sort currencies based on the export ratio using trade data for yeary−2. This is because countries report trade statistics to Comtrade slowly, sometimes with complete reports available only by the end of the following year.
Construction of these portfolios represents an implementable trading strategy, relying only on trade data available at the time of portfolio formation. Average forward discounts and average returns are computed from 01/1988 to 04/2013.
The results are reported in Panel A of TableVI. The results using both samples are very similar: portfolios representing complex-good exporters and basic-good importers (relative to their output of complex goods) have low (negative) average forward discounts, suggesting that they capture countries whose interest rates are typically low relative to the United States. Conversely, portfolios with high values of commodity exports and low values of final good exports relative to their output exhibit high average forward discounts, indicating high average interest rates. The pattern is virtually monotonic across portfolios, especially for the G10 subsample, with differences between the highest and lowest portfolios' average forward discounts of approximately 3% per annum for the full sample and about 4% per annum for the G10 sample.
Importantly, portfolio average excess returns follow the pattern of the average forward discounts. In both samples, the average excess returns (and especially the corresponding Sharpe ratios) increase virtually monotonically from about negative 25 basis points per annum for the low import ratio portfolios and up to over 4% per annum for the high import ratio portfolios. The spread in portfolio Sharpe ratios between the low and high portfolios is about one-half. Thus, differences in the average forward discounts translate almost fully into average excess returns, contrary to the UIP hypothesis.
To facilitate comparison with traditional carry trade strategies, we sort countries based on forward discounts at the same frequency as the import-sorted portfolios. That is, each January we sort currencies based on their forward discount vis-à-vis the U.S. dollar at the end of December. This puts the import sort on equal footing with the carry trade sort in the sense that the information used to update the portfolios arrives at the same frequency (in both cases, the portfolios are rebalanced monthly). The annual portfolio formation period departs from the traditional method of sorting currencies based on the current interest rate in each month as in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011). The resulting annual sort displays somewhat less variation in average forward discounts across portfolios and a narrower spread in average excess returns than the monthly sort as in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011). Average forward discounts and excess returns for these portfolios are shown in Panel B of TableVI. The spread in the average forward discounts is slightly larger in this case (from below 2% per annum for the low portfolio to above 3% per annum for the high portfolio), which is not surprising since they are the source of variation behind this sort. However, the spread in average excess returns is essentially of the same magnitude as that obtained using the import ratio sort in Panel A. This suggests that forward discounts pick up some information unrelated to risk premia, such as differences in expected inflation, and as a result, some of the spread in forward discounts is wider than the spread in average returns for this sort. Consequently, it may be that the import ratio is more informative about real interest rates and currency risk premia than forward discounts, at least in the cross section of currencies.
Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011) show that carry-sorted portfolios exhibit a strong factor structure that implies heterogeneity in countries' SDF exposures to a global source of risk, which is corroborated by the fact that average returns on carry portfolios line up with loadings on a common factor. If our model well captures such heterogeneity in risk exposures, then import-sorted portfolios should exhibit similar properties. As a candidate for the risk factorfcapturing global SDF shocks, we consider returns on a portfolio that is long the portfolio with the highest import ratio and short the portfolio with the lowest import ratio. We refer to this strategy asIMX(Importers Minus eXportersof finished goods).
TableVIIpresents results of standard asset pricing tests for the full sample of countries and the smaller G10 sample using both import-ratio and carry-sorted portfolios as test assets, to maximize the power of the tests. Panel A displays cross-sectional estimates of the IMX market price of riskλIMXand the corresponding SDF loadingbIMXusing both the SDF-GMM methodology and the Fama and MacBeth (1973) approach, together with the cross-sectional pricing error tests. Panel B presents time-series estimates of factor loadingsβIMXifor each portfolio as well as the pricing errorα0iand the joint test statistics for the alphas. The tests show that the prices of risk and factor loadings, while imprecisely estimated (given the relatively short sample length), are statistically significant (using either the efficient second-stage GMM or the Fama and MacBeth (1973) method). The estimated prices of risk are broadly consistent in magnitude with the mean of the IMX factor (4.53% in the full sample and 4.19% in the G10 sample)—they are larger but not significantly so using the first-stage GMM and Fama and MacBeth (1973) approaches, but are essentially the same using efficient GMM estimators. More importantly, the pricing errors are not statistically significant either individually or jointly using either method. Factor betas are essentially monotonically increasing in both the import ratios and the forward discounts (there is a somewhat smaller spread in betas for the latter set of assets, indicating potential measurement error problems stemming from the mismeasurement of real interest rate differentials using forward discounts). This evidence is consistent with the notion that the spread in average returns on both import-sorted and carry-sorted portfolios is driven by differences in exposure to a common source of risk captured by the IMX strategy.
As a further illustration of the model mechanism in the data, we examine the behavior of model variables during the global financial crisis, which coincided both with a dramatic decline in output, especially among final-good-producing countries, such as Japan, and a collapse in international trade volume (e.g., see Eaton et al. (2011)). As Figure9shows, the data line up nicely with the model predictions over this period. Panel A shows that the commodity currencies tended to depreciate relative to final-good-producing currencies during the crisis. Panel B illustrates that this is reflected in a large negative return on the IMX strategy, and that this return is accompanied by large negative changes in the CRB all-commodity spot index and the BDI. Perhaps, most importantly, even though commodity prices were declining during this period, Panel C shows that the productivity of the commodity countries did not fall appreciably, particularly when compared the producer countries. Panel D shows that a GDP-weighted basket of commodity countries' productivity greatly outperforms that of final-good producers during the crisis.
The evidence on the cross-sectional behavior of expected currency excess returns based on the asset pricing tests reported in SectionII.above is consistent with the unconditional implications of our model (commodity currency is riskier on average) as well as its conditional implications. The latter is because variation in a country's import ratio over time determines which portfolio it is sorted into, so that unconditional betas at the portfolio level capture conditional covariances of individual currencies with the IMX factor (Cochrane (2011) stresses this interpretation of the cross-sectional evidence in Lustig, Roussanov, and Verdelhan (2011)). However, these tests do not fully exploit our model's predictions for the conditional expected returns on the currency carry trade. Specifically, the third implication in SectionI.suggests that the conditional expected returns on the IMX strategy should vary over time, being higher when the relative productivity of commodity countries relative to producer countries is greater, and vice versa.
Testing this prediction is complicated by the fact that both our relative productivity measure and the import ratio that proxies for relative productivity in our model are time-aggregated at an annual frequency. As a consequence, they make very noisy instruments for summarizing conditioning information available at a particular point in time, especially for capturing conditional expected returns that vary over relatively short horizons. Fortunately, we can exploit our model's predictions for the behavior of trade/shipping costs to employ variables that are observed at a particular point in time—this is the last item on the list of model predictions in SectionI.. In particular, we can use the BDI of the spot freight rates for dry bulk goods available from the Baltic Exchange as a proxy for trade costs. While the model notion of trade costs is broader than just the costs of shipping, to the extent that shipping costs increase with the amount of goods exported (as the supply of ships is inelastic in the short run), they serve as a good proxy for the relative productivity that is not observed at a high-enough frequency.26Given the highly persistent nature of the BDI variable, we use its monthly growth rate,ΔBDIt+1=BDIt+1BDIt, as the conditioning variable to reduce the possibility of small-sample bias associated with persistent predictors.
We present results of our conditional tests in TableVIII. We separately consider two sets of test assets: those based on the import ratio sort and those based on the forward discount (carry) sort. The first five test assets (numbered 1 to 5) in each set are the original sorted portfolios, while the remaining portfolios (6 to 10) are the original ones scaled withΔBDIt. Panel A reports the price of risk and SDF factor loadings together with the cross-sectional tests using either the GMM or the Fama and MacBeth (1973) methodology (p-values for the appropriate χ2test statistic are reported in each case). Panel B reports the time-series regression results for each portfolio (alphas, betas, andR2) as well as the cross-sectional chi-square tests. For the import-sorted portfolios, both λ0and λ1are positive and statistically significant, indicating that not only is IMX priced in the cross section of currency returns, but also that its price of risk varies over time with shipping costs, as predicted by the model. The magnitudes are also consistent with the average returns on the corresponding factor-mimicking portfolios. The SDF factor loadingsbare generally less precisely estimated and not significant except when using the (efficient) second-stage GMM, whereb1is significant. The results are similar for the carry-sorted portfolio—the magnitudes of risk prices are somewhat larger in this case but not significantly different from the factor sample means. Crucially, in both cases, the cross-sectional pricing error tests fail to reject the model. This is consistent with the evidence from portfolio-level tests where none of the alphas are statistically significantly different from zero.
article.III. Conclusion:
We present new evidence on the currency carry trade: countries that specialize in exporting basic goods such as raw commodities tend to exhibit high interest rates, where as countries primarily exporting finished goods have lower interest rates on average. These interest rate differences translate almost entirely into average returns on currency carry-trade strategies. Our empirical findings are motivated by a theoretical model of currency risk premia that is based on technological differences between countries. We propose a novel mechanism that helps rationalize the existence of large and persistent deviations from uncovered interest rate parity in the cross section of currencies: comparative advantage in the production of different types of goods combined with convex trade costs and an inelastic trade channel. The resulting nonlinearity of trade costs, as well as the ability of the commodity country to insure itself through domestic production, imply that the SDF of the country that is more efficient at producing the consumption good is more sensitive to productivity shocks, making its currency a safe haven and the commodity country currency risky. Our model generates a number of new empirical predictions that find strong support in the data.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton

To show this graphically, in FigureA1, we bound the process below by 0.5, setσz=0.05, and simulate a sample path of one billion innovations and plot its density. The stationary density is tilted and has more mass near the upper boundary.

article.Appendix C: Exchange Rates and Commodity:
There is an alternative way to derive the relation between the exchange rate and the relative productivity wedge in6that does not require the complete solution to the planning problem but rather relies on the law of one price in goods markets (to the extent that it applies in the presence of trade costs).
EquationC1states that the marginal product of the commodity is equated between the two countries, once expressed in units of one of the country's currency (or consumption). This can be seen as a consequence of the law of one price for the commodity, since it can be transported freely between the two countries, and of perfect competition in the goods markets.
This relation must hold as long as some final good is produced in the commodity country, which is true under the assumptions maintained in Lemma1. Moreover, this relation does not depend on the assumption of complete financial markets, but rather only on the competitive structure in the goods markets. The ability to ship the commodity at no cost across countries implies that the law of one price must hold. The price of the commodity in each country must equal its marginal product using that country's technology, but the two must be equal once expressed in the same numeraire (this exchange rate conversion takes into account the cost of shipping thefinalgood).
This result may appear surprising, since the exchange rate can therefore be pinned down without the complete description of the consumption allocation above. Indeed, this is possible as long as the economy is always away from the corner solution, that is, both countries produce the final consumption good. The relative productivity differential then determines the extent to which the two countries can share risk, since in the presence of trade costs domestic production (of the final good in the commodity country) plays the same consumption-smoothing role that final good exports do. On the margin, the commodity country should be indifferent between importing one unit of the final good or producing it domestically, and the productivity differential determines the cost of the latter.
This approach evokes a popular view of commodity countries that is rooted in the terms of trade: the commodity currency appreciates (and depreciates) together with the (world) price of the country's export (the basic commodity) in relation to its import (the final consumption good).
